# Verdict

No—not unconditionally.

The new `261dc2d82` fix is correct for the Jul 13/14 `release-cli.yml` modify/delete conflict. A typical stable, numeric `desktop-v*` release is now reasonably expected to go green unattended, assuming:

- conflicts are resolvable;
- dependencies are old enough and resolvable;
- required Windows ARM64 native artifacts exist;
- Claude, npm, GitHub, and the ARM runner are available;
- upstream has not changed build/output conventions.

But several legitimate fail-closed blockers remain, plus a small number of low-probability races that can leave future nightlies unable to repair the published state automatically.

Line notation below:

- N: [nightly-merge.yml](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml:1>)
- B: [build-arm64.yml](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/.github/workflows/build-arm64.yml:1>)
- F: [FEATURES.md](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/FEATURES.md:1>)
- M: [materialize-native-closure.sh](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/scripts/materialize-native-closure.sh:1>)
- R: [check-dangerous-diagnostics.mjs](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/scripts/check-dangerous-diagnostics.mjs:1>)

## Historical fixes: verified

| Incident | Verdict | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| LOCK-REGEN | Fixed for the original Mastra failure | N70–76 pins Bun 1.3.14, matching B69–70. N249 runs `node scripts/check-override-consistency.mjs`; N256 regenerates `bun.lock`. Mastra overrides are absent. Bot merge `33fc326a2` subsequently succeeded. |
| CANDIDATE-IDEMPOTENT-PUSH | Fixed under normal branch permissions | N342 deletes the disposable candidate before N343 pushes it. Failed builds no longer poison the next ordinary run. Bot merge `33fc326a2` postdates the fix. |
| WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED | Fixed for normal workflow additions/edits | N182 restores the exact `HEAD` workflow tree; N323–327 proves no workflow delta remains. Bot merge `848414f7f` succeeded after this fix and has zero first-parent workflow-directory changes. |
| New `261dc2d82` modify/delete fix | Textually correct; not yet proven by a later successful nightly | N175–181 resolves each unmerged workflow path to `HEAD` if present, otherwise `git rm`; N182 then performs the no-overlay sweep. |

### Scrutiny of `261dc2d82`

For the actual v1.15.0 case, `release-cli.yml` is absent in fork `HEAD`, so:

```bash
git cat-file -e "HEAD:$p"   # false
git rm -q -f -- "$p"        # clears the modify/delete U entry
git checkout --no-overlay HEAD -- .github/workflows
```

That is correct. The same logic handles ordinary content conflicts, add/add, either modify/delete direction, rename targets, and rename/delete. Delete/delete normally auto-resolves before this loop. `set -euo pipefail` at N82 means failure inside the piped `while` is not swallowed.

Residual pathological cases:

- N175 uses newline-delimited `git diff --name-only`, not NUL-delimited output. Git documents that unusual pathnames are quoted/munged without `-z`; tabs, newlines, quotes, backslashes, and some non-ASCII names can therefore reach `cat-file`/`rm` as the wrong literal path. Expected error: `pathspec ... did not match any files`. Safe abort, extremely unlikely for an upstream workflow filename. [Git diff documentation](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-diff)
- A directory/file conflict such as a workflow file colliding with an upstream directory can fail during parent/child checkout ordering with `Is a directory`, `Directory not empty`, or another unmerged-path error. Safe abort, extremely unlikely.
- A rename across the `.github/workflows` boundary can materialize a conflict destination outside that pathspec. It will go to the general AI resolver, not this deterministic fork-owned sweep.

## Genuine wedges or poisoned-state classes

| Failure class | Trigger and exact boundary | Expected result | Likelihood | Classification |
|---|---|---|---:|---|
| **Queued run starts from stale `main`** | Run B is triggered while run A is active. N60–63 has no explicit `ref`, so B later checks out its event SHA, not the newly advanced `main`. B re-merges and republishes the same tag, then N388 runs `git push origin "$SHA:refs/heads/main"`. | Non-fast-forward rejection after B has replaced the release with its sibling merge. A’s baseline is already current, so the next nightly exits at N105 and does not repair the off-main release or stale candidate. | Low scheduled-only; material if manual retries overlap or a run lasts into another schedule | **Fails-broken; future no-op nightlies preserve the wrong release.** GitHub concurrency allows only one running and, by default, one pending run; checkout defaults to the event ref/SHA. [Concurrency docs](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-when-workflows-run/control-workflow-concurrency), [checkout behavior](https://github.com/actions/checkout) |

Two builds of the same version interleave B298–315. | `422 already_exists`, tag-SHA mismatch, or one build replacing the other. Worst case: nightly verifies, another build replaces the release, then nightly advances without rechecking it. | Low unless manual builds are used during nightly | **Fails-broken; baseline can advance with the wrong release.** |

| **AI removes merge state** | The resolver at N192 has unrestricted Git access. Only remaining U paths are checked; `MERGE_HEAD` is never rechecked. An accidental `git merge --abort` or reset makes the conflict check pass. | N328 bumps the baseline and N330 creates a normal single-parent commit without the upstream merge. | Extremely low | **Silent poison; upstream tag is permanently recorded as merged when it was skipped.** |
| **Candidate deletion blocked** | A ruleset/protection rejects N342, but `|| true` suppresses it. N343 then pushes a new divergent same-tag merge over the stale candidate. | Repeated non-fast-forward or GH013 rejection every night. | Configuration-dependent | **Persistent wedge until branch state/policy changes.** |
| **Direct push to `main` blocked or default branch renamed** | N388 hardcodes `refs/heads/main`. Branch protection, required PRs/signatures, or a different default branch rejects/misdirects it. | GH006/GH013, protected-branch, or non-fast-forward error after release publication. | Configuration-dependent | **Persistent wedge; release is repeatedly published ahead of the baseline.** |
| **Immutable releases enabled** | B298–306 implements delete-and-recreate. GitHub immutable releases permanently reserve a tag name after deletion. | Later `gh release create` fails, commonly with HTTP 422/tag reuse failure. | Configuration-dependent | **Permanent wedge for that version.** [GitHub immutable releases](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/concepts/supply-chain-security/immutable-releases) |
| **Tag deletion protected or transiently fails** | B299 suppresses every tag-delete error. If the old tag remains, B306’s `--target` cannot retarget it; GitHub ignores `target_commitish` for an existing tag. | `422 already_exists`, or B312–314 reports old tag SHA versus built SHA. | Configuration-dependent/low transiently | **Persistent if policy-driven; normally retryable if transient.** [Release API semantics](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/releases/releases) |
| **Same-name upstream tag is moved** | N105 compares only the tag name with the baseline before comparing object IDs. | Clean green no-op despite different upstream bytes. | Extremely low; upstream tags should be immutable | **Undetected permanently.** |
| **Tag is not numeric-stable** | N100 only accepts `desktop-v[0-9][0-9.]*`. Prerelease/suffixed tags are ignored; multiple releases between schedules collapse to the latest stable tag. | Green no-op or older stable tag selected. | Certain for prerelease tags | **Not a red run, but it does not satisfy literal “every `desktop-v*` tag.”** |
| **Schedule is delayed, dropped, or disabled** | Cron is N25 at minute zero, a documented high-load time. Public-repository schedules can also be disabled after 60 days without activity. | No run exists to report an error. | Low; inactivity case conditional | **Retry next day if dropped; persistent until re-enabled if disabled.** [GitHub schedule behavior](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/troubleshoot-workflows) |

| Fresh dependency blocked by age gate | N256 `bun install --lockfile-only`; [bunfig.toml](</C:/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64/bunfig.toml:4>) sets `minimumReleaseAge = 259200` | Medium when upstream pins a newly published package | Safe; generally succeeds after up to 72 hours. `resolve-release-age.mjs` is not invoked here. |
| Manifest/override/lock incompatibility | N249 or N256 fails. The override checker intentionally misses ranges/protocols, peer-only and purely transitive mismatches. | Low | Safe; blind spots surface later at B109 or B240 |

These become permanent only when combined with the stale-queued-run race, immutable releases, protection rules, or an external publisher race.

## False-green integrity gaps

These do not make the nightly fail; they can let it succeed incorrectly.

- The marker gate passes vacuously if the fenced ` ```markers` opening is removed or the block becomes empty. It asserts no minimum marker count.
- F60 lists the Auto-resume feature markers—`(AUTO-RESUME)`, `autoResumeManager`, `writeInputIfIdleSession`, `useAutoResumeActivity`, and `AutoResumeController`—but none appears in the machine-readable block at F74–138. That feature currently has no deterministic marker protection.
- Marker presence only proves a token exists somewhere under a broad root; dead/commented/unreachable code passes.
- Resolver scope is prompt-only. It can edit non-conflicted, unstaged, or untracked files; the reviewer is instructed to inspect `git diff --cached`, while N329 ultimately commits everything via `git add -A`.

- N294 ignores Claude’s exit status. If the reviewer writes `VERDICT: OK` first and then crashes or times out, N296 accepts the partial verdict.
- Dropped upstream workflow changes produce advisory `CI-NOTE:` lines only. They never change the verdict, so a newly required codegen/environment/package step can be omitted if the build does not expose it.
- R ignores nonmatching `tsc` failures and skips missing `tsconfig.json` paths. B269’s process-substitution `find` failure does not propagate, and the native scan does not reject every generically named x64 `.node`.
- B279–280 publishes the first matching `.exe`; multiple output executables can select the wrong asset without failing.
- There is no full test/e2e gate, an accepted fork limitation.
- Manual `force_tag` is inserted directly into shell source at N96: `FORCE='${{ github.event.inputs.force_tag }}'`. A quote-bearing dispatch input can break or inject shell. Similarly, B292–293 interpolates package/output values into shell source. Normal upstream semver and generated filenames are safe, but these API boundaries are unvalidated.

## Permission and handoff verdict

The normal token boundary is correct:

- `contents: write` is sufficient for branch, tag, and release operations.
- The reusable build cannot elevate beyond the caller, but the caller already grants the same permission.
- Build and advance consume the immutable merge SHA, not the mutable candidate branch.
- `gh release create --target "$BUILT_SHA"` and B311–315 correctly verify the tag.
- The workflow-tree restore closes the known `GITHUB_TOKEN` workflow-file push rejection for ordinary final-tree changes. GitHub separately requires a Workflows permission to edit workflow files, which the standard Actions token cannot request through workflow `permissions`. [GitHub App workflow permission](https://docs.github.com/en/apps/creating-github-apps/registering-a-github-app/choosing-permissions-for-a-github-app), [reusable-workflow permissions](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/reusing-workflow-configurations)

Historical bot pushes prove the required main/release permissions worked through July 11. Current live rulesets and immutable-release settings could not be queried from this sandbox, so the configuration-dependent classes above cannot be ruled out from repository text alone.

## Final answer

For ordinary stable upstream releases, yes: the pipeline is now reasonably expected to succeed unattended, and the specific v1.15.0 workflow modify/delete failure should be gone.

But “whenever upstream publishes any new `desktop-v*` tag” is false:

- prereleases are deliberately ignored;
- fresh dependencies can delay success up to 72 hours;
- native dependency bumps can require companion artifacts;
- real semantic or build incompatibilities intentionally stop for recovery;
- Claude, registries, GitHub, and the preview ARM runner remain live dependencies;
- low-probability queued-run, concurrent-publisher, and version-mismatch cases can leave a wrong release that future no-op nightlies do not repair.

The most likely remaining reds are safe dependency-age, service, merge/semantic, native-artifact, or build-drift aborts. The most dangerous residuals are the stale queued-event-SHA race, a concurrent standalone publisher, and the missing `NEWTAG`↔package-version invariant.

No files were modified; the worktree remained clean.

content.content:
| Subagent markers track per-marker activity | each subagent's run-dir marker is touched on every PostToolUse it fires (not just SubagentStart), so its mtime reflects last activity and the conservative 12h leaked-marker backstop can't false-green a still-working subagent; the COMMON orphan-on-API-error leak is handled fast by (API-ABORT-RELEASE) instead of a flaky short timer | `(MARKER-INACTIVE)` |
| Orphaned subagent markers reaped on API abort | a main Claude session's stream abort (`API Error: Stream idle timeout - partial response received`, an isApiErrorMessage line) ends the turn and ORPHANS in-flight subagents WITHOUT firing their SubagentStop, leaking run-dir markers that pin the dot yellow; no hook fires on the abort, so the JSONL watcher (the only thing that reads the transcript) reaps the orphaned per-subagent markers and emits review — deterministic, event-driven, no timer (survivors stay held via the next Stop's background_tasks[]) | `(API-ABORT-RELEASE)` |
| Dot state survives renderer reloads | the v2-notifications dot store persists to sessionStorage — an in-place window reload (Ctrl+R / error boundary / crash recovery) no longer wipes every dot; the background-running blue has no self-heal until the next turn end, so a reload used to hide a running background task for hours. Clears on real app restart (no stale dots across launches) | `(DOT-PERSIST)` |
| Agent hook fork-diet | every agent lifecycle hook parses + escapes JSON with bash builtins (read, `[[ =~ ]]`, parameter expansion); the old grep/tr + sed pipelines forked ~30 subprocesses per call and crashed the x64-emulated msys2 runtime on Windows ARM64 (the add_item errno-1 fork cascade that wedged every chat's hooks). Wire payload unchanged | `(HOOK-FORK-DIET)` |
| Master cards archive-only | a repo's master / non-git master card (`type === "main"`) can never be hard-removed from the sidebar — "Remove from Sidebar" archives it (recoverable under the project's Archived section) instead of hiding it into oblivion. `archiveWorkspace` inserts an archived row for an auto-included main that has none yet. The missing-worktree error page offers Archive (not Delete) for mains; the delete/complete guards resolve the workspace type host-record-first and FAIL LOUD (refuse) when the type is unresolvable instead of failing open on a cold cache; Empty Recycle Bin defensively skips mains | `(MASTER-ARCHIVE-ONLY)` |
(MARKER-INACTIVE)	apps/desktop/src/main
(API-ABORT-RELEASE)	apps/desktop/src/main
(DOT-PERSIST)	apps/desktop/src/renderer
applySourceAxes	apps/desktop/src/renderer
agent-wrappers	apps/desktop/src/main

Restore always returns to ACTIVE; nothing is ever auto-purged. Permanent git destroy is relocated to "Delete permanently" inside the bin (the old destroy dialog) + a one-confirm "Empty Recycle Bin". A renderer-only retention setting (default 30 days) is a DISPLAY filter only — the bin shows items deleted within the last N days, older ones collapse behind a per-bin "Show all" toggle with an "N hidden by filter" footer. true
# Shell scripts must stay LF. The agent hook templates under
# apps/desktop/src/main/lib/agent-setup/templates/*.sh are read VERBATIM and
# written out as the runtime hook scripts (see notify-hook.ts); a CRLF here
# would put a \r in the shebang/commands and break bash on Windows — defeating
# the (HOOK-FORK-DIET) reliability work. core.autocrlf=true must not touch them.
*.sh text eol=lf

input.command:
cd "/c/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64" && git commit -m "fix(desktop): sidebar agent chips derive from the fork dot pipeline (CHIP-DOT-UNIFY)

Upstream's workspace-agents-row chips had their own status pipe (host
binding raw lastEventType -> 4-case map) that read fork events
(SubagentActive, BackgroundRunning, compaction/teammate holds) as idle,
and a liveness filter on terminal_sessions.status - which stays 'active'
forever for a closed-tab daemon pty, trapping chips (a permission-red
chip sat 40h+ after its tab closed; every session in the live host.db
was 'active', ages to 84h).

Chips now read status from the shared (AY)/(DOT-AXES) per-source
primitive (useV2WorkspaceTerminalStatuses) and liveness from the
open-pane layout gate (useV2WorkspaceOpenTerminalIds, newly exported).
Closed tabs are unrepresentable; chips can never disagree with the
tab/pane/rollup dots. Host bindings still provide identity (logo,
label, startedAt order). Follow-up (not this commit): DockBadge,
item-actions and clear-attention still use the binding-status path."

--- a/AGENTS.md
+++ b/AGENTS.md
@@ -85,13 +85,16 @@ See `FEATURES.md` for the marker manifest. In brief:
   turn-end snapshot marker instead of false-greening).
   Precedence red > yellow > blue > green. Host-service lifecycle POSTs + a JSONL
   watcher fallback; only open tabs are represented. superset-notify.py exclusively
   owns Claude's Stop — upstream's notify.sh is deliberately NOT registered on Stop
   (its raw passthrough raced ~1s behind and wiped BackgroundRunning blue + the
   subagent yellow-hold). Every dot surface (tab, pane header, sidebar row,
-  workspace rollup, kanban card) derives from ONE per-source primitive in the
+  sidebar agent chips (CHIP-DOT-UNIFY: status from the shared primitive,
+  liveness from the open-pane gate — never `terminal_sessions.status`, which
+  stays `active` forever for closed-tab ptys), workspace rollup, kanban card)
+  derives from ONE per-source primitive in the
   v2-notifications store; the rollup is the fold of the per-source dots, so
   surfaces cannot drift. The primitive itself is layered (DOT-AXES): a source's
   status is DERIVED as the highest-precedence active axis (permission/working/
   review, plus the separate shell/background blue axes) — events latch/unlatch
   only the axes they have evidence about, so an assert can never stomp a
   higher-ranking active state; a tool that ran inside a subagent (hook payload
diff --git a/FEATURES.md b/FEATURES.md
index 068e2989f..da5e49635 100644
--- a/FEATURES.md
+++ b/FEATURES.md
@@ -51,12 +51,13 @@ in the merge that drops it (the only legitimate way a marker leaves this list).
 | Subagent StopFailure stays scoped | a StopFailure carrying an `agent_id` is ONE subagent/fork aborting on a rate-limit/API error, not the whole Claude session dying — it is handled by a SELF-SCOPED inline branch that drops ONLY that fork's run + question markers and never the shared session state (sibling markers, the main `_main` question owner, the agent-bg snapshot), and (like the main-abort branch) never trusts the abort payload's `background_tasks`; the remaining run-marker count + the `.mainstopped` sentinel decide the hold and the central red guard re-holds any still-open question. The full-abort StopFailure branch (clears run_dir + every marker + the `.askq` guard, then greens) would otherwise stomp the main loop's still-pending AskUserQuestion red when a fork hit a limit. Also: superset-notify.py now OWNS `PostToolUseFailure` (aliased to PostToolUse through the red guard) since notify.sh stopped raw-posting it | `(STOPFAIL-SUBAGENT)` |
 | Layered dot axes | a source's dot status is DERIVED as the highest-precedence active axis (permission > working > review, + the separate blue axes) — events latch/unlatch axes they have evidence about, so a lower assert can never overwrite a higher active state | `applySourceAxes` |
 | Leaked yellow-hold markers self-heal | a SubagentStop arriving with a mismatched/missing agent_id leaks its run-dir marker and pins the dot yellow with nothing running; at every Stop/SubagentStop the payload's background_tasks[] (ground truth) reaps any marker not listed as still running | `(MARKER-RECONCILE)` |
 | Subagent markers track per-marker activity | each subagent's run-dir marker is touched on every PostToolUse it fires (not just SubagentStart), so its mtime reflects last activity and the conservative 12h leaked-marker backstop can't false-green a still-working subagent; the COMMON orphan-on-API-error leak is handled fast by (API-ABORT-RELEASE) instead of a flaky short timer | `(MARKER-INACTIVE)` |
 | Orphaned subagent markers reaped on API abort | a main Claude session's stream abort (`API Error: Stream idle timeout - partial response received`, an isApiErrorMessage line) ends the turn and ORPHANS in-flight subagents WITHOUT firing their SubagentStop, leaking run-dir markers that pin the dot yellow; no hook fires on the abort, so the JSONL watcher (the only thing that reads the transcript) reaps the orphaned per-subagent markers and emits review — deterministic, event-driven, no timer (survivors stay held via the next Stop's background_tasks[]) | `(API-ABORT-RELEASE)` |
 | Dot state survives renderer reloads | the v2-notifications dot store persists to sessionStorage — an in-place window reload (Ctrl+R / error boundary / crash recovery) no longer wipes every dot; the background-running blue has no self-heal until the next turn end, so a reload used to hide a running background task for hours. Clears on real app restart (no stale dots across launches) | `(DOT-PERSIST)` |
+| Sidebar agent chips ride the fork dot pipeline | upstream's workspace-agents-row chips (agent logo + status dot under a sidebar row) originally had their own status pipe (host binding raw `lastEventType`, 4-case map that read fork events like SubagentActive/BackgroundRunning as idle) and a broken liveness filter (`terminal_sessions.status` stays `active` forever for a closed-tab pty → chips trapped permission-red for days). Chips now derive status from the shared (AY)/(DOT-AXES) per-source primitive and liveness from the open-pane layout gate — a closed tab's chip is unrepresentable, and a chip can never disagree with the tab/pane/rollup dots | `(CHIP-DOT-UNIFY)` |
 | Agent hook fork-diet | every agent lifecycle hook parses + escapes JSON with bash builtins (read, `[[ =~ ]]`, parameter expansion); the old grep/tr + sed pipelines forked ~30 subprocesses per call and crashed the x64-emulated msys2 runtime on Windows ARM64 (the add_item errno-1 fork cascade that wedged every chat's hooks). Wire payload unchanged | `(HOOK-FORK-DIET)` |

+ *    `lastEventType`, whose 4-case mapping read the fork's event vocabulary
+ *    (`SubagentActive`, `BackgroundRunning`, …) as idle.
+ * The host binding still provides identity: which agent (logo/label) and
+ * `startedAt` ordering.
  */
 export function useDashboardSidebarWorkspaceRunningAgents(
 	workspaceId: string,
 	enabled = true,
 ): DashboardSidebarRunningAgent[] {
 	const bindings = useTerminalAgentBindings(workspaceId, { enabled });
-	const statuses = useTerminalAgentStatuses(workspaceId, { enabled });
+	const openTerminalIds = useV2WorkspaceOpenTerminalIds(workspaceId);
+	const terminalStatuses = useV2WorkspaceTerminalStatuses(workspaceId);
 
 	return useMemo(() => {
+		const statusByTerminal = new Map<string, DisplayStatus>();
+		for (const entry of terminalStatuses) {
+			statusByTerminal.set(entry.terminalId, entry.status);
+		}
 		const agents: DashboardSidebarRunningAgent[] = [];
 		for (const binding of bindings.values()) {
+			if (!openTerminalIds.has(binding.terminalId)) continue;
 			agents.push({
 				sourceKey: `terminal:${binding.terminalId}`,
 				source: { type: "terminal", id: binding.terminalId },
 				terminalId: binding.terminalId,
 				agentId: binding.agentId,
-				status: statuses.get(binding.terminalId) ?? "idle",
+				status: statusByTerminal.get(binding.terminalId) ?? "idle",
 				startedAt: binding.startedAt,
 				label: BUILTIN_AGENT_LABELS[binding.agentId] ?? binding.agentId,
 			});
 		}
 		agents.sort((a, b) => a.startedAt - b.startedAt);
 		return agents;
-	}, [bindings, statuses]);
+	}, [bindings, openTerminalIds, terminalStatuses]);
 }

`(none)`

/code-review low: clean. Codex xhigh review running in background; will act on its verdict when it lands. Status of everything in flight:

- **Chip fix committed locally** (`5f8493a0b`, not pushed — holding until the nightly's advance job finishes so I don't break its fast-forward).
- **Your stale-chip question answered:** closing a tab never disposes the terminal — the pty lives on in the daemon, `terminal_sessions.status` stays `active` forever, and the binding freezes at its last event (`PermissionRequest` = red). Every closed-tab-with-agent accumulates one; once a workspace has ≥2, the chip strip appears. The fix makes chips use your open-tabs gate, so a closed tab's chip disappears instantly, and its dot is your dot.
- **Nightly run 29375661214** still in progress (monitor armed).
- **Codex Ultra pipeline verdict** (from earlier): fix confirmed correct; typical releases should now go green unattended; flagged 3 residual risks worth hardening — queued-run stale-checkout race, standalone-build/nightly publish race, and a missing `NEWTAG`↔package-version assert — plus cheap items (validate `force_tag` input, AUTO-RESUME markers absent from FEATURES.md machine block, vacuous-pass guard on the marker gate). I'll propose that hardening batch once the current run completes.

content.text: Adding the version↔tag assert (before the commit step):

input.old_string: # --- record new baseline + commit the merge -------------------------
input.new_string:
# --- record new baseline + commit the merge -------------------------
          # (NIGHTLY-INTEGRITY) The release tag is derived SOLELY from
          # apps/desktop/package.json by the build — if the merged version
          # doesn't match the tag being merged, the build would delete and
          # rebuild the WRONG release (e.g. a bad package.json conflict
          # resolution keeping 1.14.3 while merging desktop-v1.15.0), then
          # advance the baseline past a version that no longer has a correct
          # release. Assert the invariant here instead.
          VERSION=$(node -p "require('./apps/desktop/package.json').version")
          if [ "desktop-v${VERSION}" != "$NEWTAG" ]; then
            echo "::error::merged apps/desktop/package.json version ($VERSION) does not match $NEWTAG — bad version resolution; refusing to commit. Baseline untouched."
            exit 1
          fi

# scratch (never a feature/history branch), so overwriting it is safe;
          # we delete-then-push rather than --force a real branch.
          git push origin --delete "$CAND" 2>/dev/null || true
          git push origin "$CAND"

          VERSION=$(node -p "require('./apps/desktop/package.json').version")
          echo "should_build=true" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "newtag=$NEWTAG" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "version=$VERSION" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "candidate=$CAND" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          # (NIGHTLY-INTEGRITY) build + advance consume this immutable SHA, not
          # the mutable branch name — a replaced candidate can't be built or
          # advanced unnoticed.
          echo "sha=$(git rev-parse HEAD)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

input.command:
cd "/c/Users/khair/Code Projects/fork-superset-windows-arm64" && git add .github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml FEATURES.md && git cat-file -p ":.github/workflows/nightly-merge.yml" | grep -c $'\r'; git commit -m "ci(nightly): harden pipeline per Codex Ultra review (NIGHTLY-INTEGRITY)

Four residual failure classes from the external ultra-effort pipeline
review, all deterministic:

- Pin the merge job checkout to ref: main. A queued run otherwise checks
  out its trigger-time event SHA (pre-advance main), re-merges the
  already-merged tag into a stale tree, replaces the fresh release with a
  sibling merge, and dies non-fast-forward after publishing — the worst
  fails-broken residual (future no-op nightlies never repair it).
- Assert desktop-v<package.json version> == NEWTAG before committing:
  the build derives the release tag solely from package.json, so a bad
  version conflict resolution would delete + rebuild the WRONG release
  and advance the baseline past a version with no correct release.
- Validate the force_tag dispatch input (^desktop-v[0-9][0-9.]*\$) at the
  boundary — it was interpolated into the script unvalidated.
- Marker gate: fail loud when the FEATURES.md markers block is missing or
  empty (a renamed/emptied block previously passed vacuously), and add
  the 5 AUTO-RESUME markers that were listed in prose but absent from
  the machine-readable block (that feature had zero merge protection).

Verified locally: YAML parses, bash -n clean, all 69 markers grep-hit
their roots, non-empty guard passes."
